speaker: interviewer
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's important.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Irene Dansky. The date is August 20, 1996. We are in Elkins Park, Pennsylvania, near Philadelphia. We are interviewing Liesl Joseph Loeb, and the language is English. Tell us your name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liesl Joseph Loeb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-E-S-L, Joseph, J-O-S-E-P-H, Loeb, L-O-E-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liesl Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Have you had any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: During World War II, I dropped the Liesl and became Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other nicknames or Hebrew or Yiddish names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Hebrew name is Esther, Esther [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is June 17, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I do have another nickname. My husband called me that and my grandchildren call me that. It's Schatzi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it have any special meaning or is it an abbreviation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It means 'little treasure,' but because my husband used to call me that, the grandchildren are continuing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small city in West Germany, in the Rhineland, called Rheydt. It's spelled R-H-E-Y-D-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: During your childhood, who lived in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, and myself, and in my early childhood also a nanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes my father married late his life. He was 45 when he married. My mother was 26 when she married or 27. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father was my buddy. He was my friend. There was never any generation gap. He was my hero. He was a lawyer. He had a firm in our city. He represented industrial concerns, as well as the usual thing lawyers do, such as divorce. One of the cases that really brought him to the attention of the public was an abortion case with two doctors. And I think after that, his practice really began to flourish. He had a partner. And he was very active with B'nai B'rith. He was politically a Social Democrat, which did not become very popular as time went on. And he was a wonderful, wonderful husband and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josef Joseph, Josef with an F and Joseph with a PH.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he always an attorney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, right before World War I, he sat as a justice in the courts in-- I believe in Cologne. And that actually was the reason why after the Nuremberg laws stopped Jews from having-- practicing their professions, he was eventually reinstated, because he was already in that field before World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did he start practicing law?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe his-- he was accepted to the bar in 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he have to stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was a Jew. I found in our papers a letter where he requests that he be permitted to represent at least Jews only until the end of 1938. And that was denied. He-- the order for him to-- to decease-- to cease practicing came right after Crystal Night, which was in November of '38. And that was-- that was the end of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came-- was the youngest of three sisters. I think she was the most beautiful of the three. She was a trained coloratura soprano singer. And she used to entertain in Jewish circles sometimes, singing and play the guitar. And she-- her father did not permit her to study. She wanted to study medicine or she wanted to study art. And he just wanted her to stay home, look pretty, and play the piano. So that's what she did. She-- she enjoyed traveling. And basically, she had no particular career of any kind. She got married at-- I think in-- they got married in 1927, so she was 26 years old. And she ran a big household. And she was a wonderful wife to my father and a very caring mother. And she was in times of crisis a Goliath. And then afterwards, she'd break down. And there were many times when her strength was called upon in her lifetime.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your childhood, were there specific moments you can think of that that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. For instance, when I was six, I had a very difficult middle ear infection and a consequent operation, mastoid operation. And she stayed with me in the hospital for six weeks. She stayed with me in the same room, took care of me. I would say when things-- things more seriously happened, when political situations changed, for instance during Crystal Night, my mother and myself, a visitor, and a young domestic were in our house. And she kept her head and took us to the apartment of tenants that we had in the house to-- to-- to save us. My father was in jail at the time. After the house was destroyed, the next day she had the presence of mind to have pictures taken of the house. She boarded it up. And she took me to friends who were Dutch nationals and where she felt I would be safe. Then she went back to my hometown to try and get my father out of jail. And eventually, she was able to accomplish that too, by meeting with a policeman who called her one night to meet her in some dark alley. He had news of my father. And even though she was scared, she went to meet him. And she gave the policeman money. She gave him medicine and fresh clothes for my father. And eventually, through this liaison, my father was released. And again, in London, when we were in England, much further away--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait on it. And we'll do that when we get to London.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. But she-- she showed her strength numerous times, never, never ever forgetting who she was. And that was one of the points that I admired her for so much, that she was really an inspiration to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to before the war, and what was life like in your town? What was the town like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first 10 years, first of all, of my childhood were really very happy, very secure. I was an only child. And I knew-- I didn't have any problems of sibling rivalry. I knew I was loved. I enjoyed being wherever children were. Our town was actually also the birthplace of Joseph Goebbels, who became the propaganda minister in the Hitler regime. Our town, everybody knew everybody else. Everybody knew my father, first of all, because he was-- had a well-known law firm, I would say probably one of the most successful, perhaps raising envy on the part of some colleagues, especially non-Jewish colleagues. And life was good. We lived in a big house. I had-- I went to a Jewish day school, so that I did not suffer some of the early anti-Semitic situations that other children experienced who did not go to Jewish day schools and who were eventually thrown out of their schools. And-- and my playmates, naturally, were also from school. And with the exception of neighborhood children, who started to call me all kinds of names when I was four, five years old, I didn't really have too many negative experiences as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a town was Rheydt?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, approximately 70,000. We were very close to the next town, which was Monchengladbach. Today that's all one municipality. But at that time, these were separate towns with separate Jewish congregations and separate synagogues and so on. But for instance, there was a Jewish athletic club that I belonged to. And I would travel once a week to Monchengladbach to-- to do athletic activities and meet other Jewish children. There was a Jewish community center there where that took place. I think it was a close-knit community, Jewish community specifically. But my father had also in the beginning many non-Jewish clients. He was well known.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was he active politically in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, he was a known Social Democrat. I'm not quite sure what these activities entailed. But it was a known fact and he was on a blacklist once the Nazis came into power. There was a-- when Chamberlain came to Germany to-- before the Czechoslovakian take-over, in case the conference would not have resulted in supposed peace, there was a blacklist of people who would have been hanged in the public square. And my father was on the top of the list as a Social Democrat and a Jew. That-- this is what my mother told me. I really-- I think perhaps he wrote articles in periodicals, Social Democratic periodicals. But I really have no other knowledge of what his activities entailed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Politically, that is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know Goebbels in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did know Goebbels very well. When Goebbels first graduated university, he wanted to be a playwright. And he wrote a play which was declared a plagiarized plot. And so he was discouraged and often didn't have financial means for his next meal. And very often my father would have him at his table. And give-- perhaps steer him into a direction that would be feasible for himself. Goebbels was a cripple, you know. He had a club foot. He was of very small stature. And even though he was quite brilliant and a brilliant speaker, I think that gave him some kind of a complex. And once the Nazis came to power, they took advantage of his mind and he was able to further himself in the Party. That's-- that's I think how that happened that he became what he was. But in his student and post-student years was a whole different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was that? Do you have any idea the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in the early '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- was there any contact with him later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was not. But my father here in Philadelphia was translating editor for a German newspaper, which appeared daily here in Philadelphia, the And he wrote an open letter to Joseph Goebbels at that time. That was the only other indication of the relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there-- you had a nanny--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the household.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was like my second mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Ottie Hagedorn. And when-- well, actually, that was her married name. Her maiden name was Ottie [GERMAN]. And her parents were like my grandparents. Her father used to take me to ride on the carousel when it came around. And her mother used to spoil me with all kinds of knitted things that she made for me. I had a grandmother, but these two people were something like grandparents. And when I started school, she didn't-- no longer stayed-- stayed my-- my nanny. And she got married just before I started school. And she insisted that I carry her veil in her church wedding. This was already during the Hitler time and my mother felt that it might not be advisable both for her and for us. And she said, oh, she wouldn't get married in church unless I would carry her veil, so I carried her veil. She used to come and see me afterwards. She didn't live in our town anymore. She moved to Essen with her husband. And whenever she came home to visit her parents, she'd come and visit me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Party asked her not to do that anymore, the Nazi Party. And she told them she's going to do whatever she wants. And they said, well, could you visit them at nights? And she said, no. I'm going to visit whenever I want. And so she did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she probably lost track of us once we left Germany, because not knowing what happened to the passengers of the finally when the war was over, I wrote to the same address where I knew she lived before and we resumed our relationship until she died. She came to see me in this country, and it was like before. I was her kid. She used to call me 'the kid' in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much contact with your own grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only had a grandmother. And of course, my grandmother lived with my mother's older sister in the-- in her own house. It was my grandmother's house and my mother's older sister lived in the same house. It was a big house. It was in the next little town, where my mother was actually born. And my grandmother came usually every Saturday afternoon. And my mother would invite some of her friends over for coffee. And sometimes she'd stay overnight, and sometimes she didn't. And later on, when my mother's older sister left Germany, my grandmother moved into our house and she stayed with us. Now, there was another sister who lived in Berlin. She was a doctor. And my grandmother would visit there occasionally, for a period of maybe a couple of months, and then come back to us again. During Crystal Night, she happened to be in Berlin, and that saved her life, because the Nazis came in through-- the vandals came in through her room and ripped open the mattress with a knife in her room. So she was luckily at the time in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Mathilda Salmon, and her maiden name was Heiman. She came from Kaiserslautern. Want me to spell that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's K-A-I-S-E-R-S-L-A-U-T-E-R-N. It's in-- I think it's-- it's southern Germany. And she was one of 12 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your aunts' names? Your mother's sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sisters? Johanna was the oldest. And Esthel was the middle one, the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did not. Johanna and her husband, foolish Ulrich, U-L-R-I-C-H, LAST name Heidelberger, H-E-I-D-E-L-B-E-R-G-E-R-, they emigrated to Holland. My-- my uncle ran my grandfather's factory. My grandfather had a clothing factory and my uncle took it over when my grandfather died. And he was warned that the Nazis were coming to confiscate the factory. And the night before, my uncle walked across the border into Holland. And later on, my aunt followed. Unfortunately, they were caught by the invasion of the Nazis and they were sent to Sobibor and did not survive. They had one daughter, Ilse, and she came to this country alone in 1936 or '37. My mother's sister lived in Berlin. Her husband was a musician. She had one child, Gunther, a boy, very gifted. And they had very high quota numbers. They never made it. They were deported to the Lodz ghetto. And from there, I have documentation that they were deported further. And there is a word in the book that the Germans have published regarding all the departations from Germany, you know. And in that book is a word that says verschollen. And verschollen means disappeared, so that unfortunately we have no further knowledge of how they perished, but they perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Rheydt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left in May of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: What what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Well, it was past Crystal Night. My father, I think-- up until 1938, I think he thought that-- like so many other Jewish intelligentsia that this folly could not last, that it-- it had to-- it had to stop. I think he was-- I don't know what convinced him, at what point he was convinced. I know it was before Crystal Night, because of the correspondence I'm finding regarding our immigration. There was, for instance, the dismissal or the deportation of all aliens, Jewish aliens out of Germany in approximately October of 1938 to no man's land between Germany and Poland. I don't know if it happened before that. But at one-- some point, I think in 1938, he was convinced it was time to go. It was time to get out. And I found all kinds of correspondence to that effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, there was Kristallnacht. And after Kristallnacht, I-- my mother took me to Bonn, to friends who were Dutch nationals, and therefore the house was Dutch property. And she felt that I would be safe there. And through these friends, my parents met a Cuban family by the name of Stutzel, S-T-U-E-T-Z-E-L. He was German, but a Cuban-- had become a Cuban citizen. And his wife was Cuban. And through their efforts, my parents got these immigration permits into Cuba. They were visiting Cuba and they heard about it, that the immigration official in Havana was issuing immigration permits. And they-- they bought them for my parents and brought them back. And so then, the next step was to find a ways of leaving Germany. Meanwhile, our relatives in the United States, who up until Crystal Night had felt my father was too old to emigrate into the United States, considering his occupation and his age, had issued us affidavits, which is a guarantee that they would support us in case it was necessary, and so on. And all we needed now was somewhere to go and some way to get there. And the was like a godsend when the Germans declared they were preparing a ship that would leave for Cuba in May of 1939, and that it would be specifically for Jewish refugees who wanted to leave Germany. And so my father immediately got in touch with a travel agency and booked passage. And-- and that's how we came to be on the Louis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the how-- you heard about the just through--

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I don't know how they heard about it. Perhaps it was publicized in the paper or perhaps it was through these Cuban friends. I don't know how they heard about it. But there it was, you know? And the minute they heard it-- sometimes these thing go by word of mouth when somebody hears about something like that, because there were such few ships that took Jews all together. In my papers, for instance, I found out that most ships which left Germany at that time in history had only a limited amount of space that they reserved for Jews, so that even if you had all the necessary papers to leave, finding a ship to take you, a means of getting out, was still a problem. And here was a ship that was entirely for Jewish refugees, so that was quite something. Of course, there was an ulterior motive for the Germans. And this motive really is one that we didn't find out until the authors of the book found evidence that this was to be a-- a trip for an espionage situation, and the Germans figured, let the Jews pay for it. That was something none of us knew at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people ordinarily-- how did Jews ordinarily go about getting affidavits, documentation, permission to leave the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- it was a very complicated matter to do this. First of all, the American consulates had been instructed, as we now know, not to make it too easy for people-- for Jews to enter the United States. And since there-- the German quota for immigration into the States at that time was 25,000 per year, 25,000 people per year. And the government made no allowances barring from other quotas that was it. And we now also have found out that those quotas were never even filled, even though at that time we didn't know it. There was such an onslaught for requests that then the consulate issued numbers, first come, first serve numbers, like in a delicatessen store. This was never an official announcement. It spread over Germany by word of mouth. I know my parents had friends-- excuse me-- [DRINKS] with whom they got together regularly, who by very casual conversation said, well, there is now a quota system. You have-- you have to get numbers in-- before you can go to the consulate. Our consulate was in Stuttgart. You have to apply for a number. And immediately, my mother would call up her sister in Berlin and say, listen, you've got to get a number. My parents had a fairly low number. It was-- I have the documentation. It was in the 14,000s. That was considered a low number. By the time her sister was able to get to a consulate and get a number, they were in the 70,000s. And that was like a condemnation to death. And you could not-- you could not do anything without that number insofar as coming into the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that true for other countries too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each had a quota.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my parents had an affidavit by the-- by the beginning of 1939, and they had visa. And all they needed was the passage, the money to pay for it. And they were going to wait for their quota number in another country. And that's what most of the passengers on the were doing. They did not intend to stay in Cuba, but simply wait far away from Germany, outside of Germany, until their quota numbers would come up.
